'''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）は[[能楽]]の流派の一。
{{db|定義未満}} '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）は[[能楽]]の流派の一。

revision.text: #REDIRECT[[能#金剛流]]

====金剛流==== [[法隆寺]]に仕えた猿楽座である坂戸座を源流とする流派。坂戸孫太郎氏勝が流祖。六世三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」とも言われ、写実性に富んだ大胆さも見える。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。
1936年金剛右京の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である野村金剛家（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家継承。現宗家は孫の[[金剛永謹]]で五流宗家の中で唯一京都に在住する。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことでも有名。近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]、その後[[豊嶋三千春]]がいる。

{{出典の明記}} '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。シテ方の五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住である。
==概要== [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」とも言われる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]、その後[[豊嶋三千春]]がいる。
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>
==注== <references />

1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]、その後[[豊嶋三千春]]がいる。
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>

{{出典の明記}} '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。シテ方の五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住である。
==概要== [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。

1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]、その後[[豊嶋三千春]]がいる。
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>

{{出典の明記}} '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。シテ方の五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住である。
==概要== [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。なお、江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。また幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>
== その他 == 江戸時代の[[米沢藩]]藩主で、[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）の養父、[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を愛好しており、このため治憲は[[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）に当時の宗家の金剛三郎を、隠居して米沢で生活していた重定の元に招いている。
==注== <references />
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ 金剛能楽堂] * [http://www.geocities.jp/yogilabo/ 今井克紀の電能舞台]
[[Category:能の流派|*4]]

豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。なお、江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。また幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]は芝飯倉に在住していた。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。

== その他 == 江戸時代の[[米沢藩]]藩主で、[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）の養父、[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を愛好しており、このため治憲は[[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）に当時の宗家の金剛三郎を、隠居して米沢で生活していた重定の元に招いている。

{{出典の明記}} [[ファイル:Kongo7550.JPG|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。シテ方の五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住である。
==概要== [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。なお、江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。また幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は芝飯倉に在住していた。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
金剛能楽堂をもつ（京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル、座席数 定席412席（補助席80席））
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>
== その他 == 江戸時代の[[米沢藩]]藩主で、[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）の養父、[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を愛好しており、このため治憲は[[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）に当時の宗家の金剛三郎を、隠居して米沢で生活していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士にも広がった。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
==注== <references />
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ 金剛能楽堂] * [http://www.geocities.jp/yogilabo/ 今井克紀の電能舞台] *[http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ 廣田幸稔]
{{DEFAULTSORT:こんこうりゆう}} [[Category:能の流派|*4]]

{{出典の明記|date=2008年4月}} [[ファイル:Kongo7550.JPG|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。シテ方の五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住である。

近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
金剛能楽堂をもつ（京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル、座席数 定席412席（補助席80席））
==井上流との関係== 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>
== その他 == 江戸時代の[[米沢藩]]藩主で、[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）の養父、[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を愛好しており、このため治憲は[[安永]]7年([[1778年]]）に当時の宗家の金剛三郎を、隠居して米沢で生活していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士にも広がった。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。

{{出典の明記|date=2008年4月}} [[ファイル:Kongo7550.JPG|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。
== 概要 == [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。江戸中期の[[米沢藩]]では8代藩主[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を長く愛好していた。[[安永]]7年（[[1778年]]）、9代藩主[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）は当時宗家の[[金剛三郎]]を隠居していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士のあいだにも金剛流が広がったという。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は江戸の芝飯倉に在住していた。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 京舞井上流との関係 == 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>

江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は江戸の芝飯倉に在住していた。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 京舞井上流との関係 == 京舞[[井上流]]の家元、二世[[井上八千代]]は金剛流の舞を学び、井上流のその要素を取り入れた。こうしたことから、金剛流は[[観世流]]の片山九郎右衛門家などと並び、井上流との関係が深い。金剛流能楽師と井上流の舞い手が夫婦となっている例もある。<ref>[http://www.cocokara-network.com/IkiIki.html 廣田幸稔さん「金剛流能楽師」]</ref>
== 関連項目 == * [[観世流]] * [[宝生流]] * [[金春流]] * [[喜多流]]
== 参考文献 == * 松田存著『能・狂言入門』文研出版、1976年

[[ファイル:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。
== 概要 == [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。江戸中期の[[米沢藩]]では8代藩主[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を長く愛好していた。[[安永]]7年（[[1778年]]）、9代藩主[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）は当時宗家の[[金剛三郎]]を隠居していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士のあいだにも金剛流が広がったという。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は江戸の芝飯倉に在住していた。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）

== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。

[[ファイル:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。
== 概要 == [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流の中で唯一独自の謡本を刊行することがなかった。江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。江戸中期の[[米沢藩]]では8代藩主[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を長く愛好していた。[[安永]]7年（[[1778年]]）、9代藩主[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）は当時宗家の[[金剛三郎]]を隠居していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士のあいだにも金剛流が広がったという。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は江戸の[[芝 (東京都港区)|芝]][[飯倉 (東京都港区)|飯倉]]に在住していた。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。
== 参考文献 == * 松田存著『能・狂言入門』文研出版、1976年。
== 関連項目 == * [[観世流]] * [[宝生流]] * [[金春流]] * [[喜多流]]

幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。
== 参考文献 == * 松田存著『能・狂言入門』文研出版、1976年。

[[ファイル:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。
== 概要 == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|金剛流発祥之地碑（[[龍田神社]]境内）]] [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、室町初期の[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流（[[観世流|観世]]・[[宝生流|宝生]]・[[金春流|金春]]・金剛・[[喜多流|喜多]]）の中で唯一独自の[[謡本]]を刊行することがなかった。江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。江戸中期の[[米沢藩]]では8代藩主[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を長く愛好していた。[[安永]]7年（[[1778年]]）、9代藩主[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）は当時宗家の[[金剛三郎]]を隠居していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士のあいだにも金剛流が広がったという。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。[[幕末]]の宗家は江戸の[[芝 (東京都港区)|芝]][[飯倉 (東京都港区)|飯倉]]に在住していた。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。

1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の[[金剛巌 (初世)|金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。
近年の名人としては[[金剛謹之輔]]、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]がいる。
現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。
== 参考文献 == * 松田存著『能・狂言入門』文研出版、1976年。
== 関連項目 == * [[観世流]] * [[宝生流]] * [[金春流]] * [[喜多流]]

[[ファイル:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|金剛能楽堂、京都市上京区]] '''金剛流'''（こんごうりゅう）とは、日本の[[伝統芸能]]である[[能楽]]のうち、[[能]]のシテ方の流派の一つである。現在の宗家は[[金剛永謹]]。
== 概要 == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|金剛流発祥之地碑（[[龍田神社]]境内）]] [[法隆寺]]に仕えた[[猿楽]]座である坂戸座を源流とする流派で、室町初期の[[坂戸孫太郎氏勝]]を流祖とする。六世の三郎正明から金剛を名乗る。華麗・優美な芸風から「舞金剛」、装束や面の名品を多く所蔵することから「面金剛」とも呼ばれる。
豪快な芸風で知られた七世[[金剛氏正]]は「鼻金剛」の異名を取り、中興の祖とされる。しかし、室町から江戸期においては他流に押されて振るわず、五流（[[観世流|観世]]・[[宝生流|宝生]]・[[金春流|金春]]・金剛・[[喜多流|喜多]]）の中で唯一独自の[[謡本]]を刊行することがなかった。江戸初期に金剛流から[[喜多流]]が分派している。江戸中期の[[米沢藩]]では8代藩主[[上杉重定]]が金剛流を長く愛好していた。[[安永]]7年（[[1778年]]）、9代藩主[[上杉治憲]]（上杉鷹山）は当時宗家の[[金剛三郎]]を隠居していた重定の元に招いている。これ以後、米沢藩士のあいだにも金剛流が広がったという。現代でも、京都を拠点とする金剛流で、東京以北に流派の団体があるのは米沢だけである。
幕末から明治にかけて活躍した[[金剛唯一]]は『[[土蜘蛛]]』の千筋の糸を考案したことで知られる。[[幕末]]には江戸の[[芝 (東京都港区)|芝]][[飯倉 (東京都港区)|飯倉]]に在住していた。江戸時代の[[石高]]は100石。
1936年[[金剛右京]]の死去により、坂戸金剛家は断絶。翌1937年、他の四流の家元の推薦により、弟子家筋である[[野村金剛家]]（京都金剛家）の名人で関西能楽界の重鎮として知られていた[[金剛謹之輔]]の子・[[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]]が金剛流家元となり、宗家を継承。以降の名人としては、[[豊嶋弥左衛門]]、[[金剛巌 (2世)|二世金剛巌]]らがいる。現在の宗家は、1998年9月18日に金剛流二十六世宗家を継承した金剛永謹である。
シテ方五流の宗家の中で唯一[[京都]]在住であり、'''金剛能楽堂'''を所有している。 :所在地：京都市上京区烏丸通中立売上ル 金剛能楽堂 （座席数 定席412席、補助席80席）
== 宗家代々 == *十四世 [[金剛緑勝]] *十五世 [[金剛長頼]] **十四世の子。 *十七世 [[金剛弥市久則]] *十八世 [[金剛三郎氏福]] **十七世の長男。 *十九世 [[金剛氏但]] **十七生の次男、のちに兄の十八世の養子。 *二十世 [[金剛三郎氏栄]]
*二十一世 [[金剛唯一]] **二十世の次男。 *二十二世 [[金剛氏善]] **二十一世の長男。 *二十三世 [[金剛右京]] **二十二世の長男。 *二十四世 [[金剛巌 (初世)|初世金剛巌]] **[[金剛謹之輔]]の次男。 *二十五世 [[金剛巌 (二世)|二世金剛巌]] **二十四世の三男。 *二十六世 [[金剛永謹]] **二十五世の長男。
== 参考文献 == * 松田存著『能・狂言入門』文研出版、1976年。
== 関連項目 == * [[観世流]] * [[宝生流]] * [[金春流]] * [[喜多流]]

revision.comment: Created page with "[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹). == Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origin..."
revision.text:
[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] ** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] ** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] ** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] ** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] ** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] ** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] ** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] ** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] ** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] ** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] ** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] ** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] ** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] ** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] ** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] ** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] ** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] ** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] ** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] ** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] ** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] ** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] ** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] ** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] ** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] ** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] ** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] ** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] ** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] ** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] ** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] ** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] ** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] ** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] ** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] ** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] *** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] *** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] *** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] *** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] *** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] *** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] *** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] *** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] *** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] *** Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] *** Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] *** Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] *** Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] *** Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] *** Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] *** Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] *** Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] *** Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

[[ja:金剛流]]

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

[[ja:金剛流]]

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

[[ja:金剛流]]

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

[[ja:金剛流]]

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the [[shite]]-kata style in [[Noh]], a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of [[sarugaku]] performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early [[Muromachi period]]. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}}, {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|ja|米沢藩}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7年}}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|ja|上杉治憲}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|ja|土蜘蛛}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Shiba|ja|芝}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in [[Kyoto]] and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shuppan, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze|ja|観世流}} * {{ill|Hōshō|ja|宝生流}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota Appreciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio
