<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646539]] Wikidata name: Kongō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646539]] Wikidata name: Kongō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:17, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the {{ill|shite|en|shite|WD=Q7499149|lt=shite}}-kata style in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}}, a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuta Shrine|qq=}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}} performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigetada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|WD=Q207766|lt=Shiba}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shu11an, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze}} * {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota A11reciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]]
[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the {{ill|shite|en|shite|WD=Q7499149|lt=shite}}-kata style in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}}, a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuta Shrine|qq=}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku}} performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigetada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|WD=Q207766|lt=Shiba}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shu11an, 1976.
== See Also == * {{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze}} * {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō}} * {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}} * {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}
== External Links == * [http://www.nohgaku.or.jp/ Nōgaku Kyōkai Official Website] * [http://www.kongou-net.com/ Kongō Nōgakudō Official Website] ; Kongō Nōgakushi *[http://www.kongou-net.com/ryumonnokai/ Kongō Ryūkin Official Website] Kongō Ryūkin * [http://www.imaikatsunori.com/ Imai Katsunori's Electronic Nō Stage] Imai Katsunori * [http://hirota-kansyokai.la.coocan.jp/ Hirota A11reciation Society] Hirota Yukitoshi * {{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.jp/tatsushige3/ |title=Udaka Tatsushige Official Site |date=20181105024610}} Udaka Tatsushige * {{Wayback|url=http://junseikai.cool.ne.jp/ |title=Junsei-kai |date=20100101000000}} Yamada Sumio

== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]]
[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the {{ill|shite|en|shite|WD=Q7499149|lt=shite|1=Shite}}-kata style in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}, a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuta Shrine|qq=}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}} performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze|1=Kanze (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō|1=Hōshō (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain|1=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigesada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|WD=Q207766|lt=Shiba|1=Lawn}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|WD=Q207766|lt=Shiba|1=Lawn}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shu11an, 1976.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kongō (Nō school)]][[ja:金剛流]]
[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the {{ill|shite|en|shite|WD=Q7499149|lt=shite|1=Shite}}-kata style in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}, a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuta Shrine|qq=}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|WD=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}} performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|WD=Q4525037|lt=Kanze|1=Kanze (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|WD=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō|1=Hōshō (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain|1=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigesada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|WD=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|WD=Q207766|lt=Shiba|1=Lawn}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|qid=Q4525037|lt=Kanze|1=Kanze (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|qid=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō|1=Hōshō (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|qid=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain|1=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigesada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|qid=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|qid=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|qid=Q207766|lt=Shiba|1=Lawn}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>

== Further Reading == * Matsuda Son. ''Introduction to Nō and Kyōgen.'' Bunken Shu11an, 1976.

[[File:Kongo_Noh_Theatre.jpg|thumb|220px|Kongō Nōgakudō, Kyoto]] '''Kongō School''' (金剛流) is a school of the {{ill|shite|en|shite|qid=Q7499149|lt=shite|1=Shite}}-kata style in {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|qid=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}}, a traditional Japanese performing art. The current head of the school is [[Kongō Hisanori]] (金剛永謹).
== Overview == [[File:Tatsuta-Jinja, Kongoryu-sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|Monument marking the birthplace of the Kongō School (within {{ill|Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Taisha|lt=Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuta Shrine|qq=}})]] The Kongō School traces its origins to the Sakato-za, a troupe of {{ill|Sarugaku|en|Sarugaku|qid=Q1204318|lt=Sarugaku|1=Sarugaku}} performers serving {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|qid=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}}. It was founded by Sakato Magotarō Ujikatsu during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. From the sixth generation leader, Saburō Masaaki, the name Kongō was adopted. Known for its elegant and refined performance style, it earned the nickname "Mai Kongō" ("Dance Kongō"). Due to its collection of exquisite costumes and masks, it is also referred to as "Omote Kongō" ("Mask Kongō").
The seventh-generation head, [[Kongō Ujinari]], renowned for his bold performance style, was nicknamed "Hana Kongō" ("Nose Kongō") and is considered the school's reviver. However, from the Muromachi to Edo periods, the school struggled against other schools. It was the only one among the five major schools ({{ill|Kanze (Noh school)|en|Kanze (Noh school)|qid=Q4525037|lt=Kanze|1=Kanze (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Hōshō (Noh school)|en|Hōshō (Noh school)|qid=Q11452637|lt=Hōshō|1=Hōshō (Noh school)}}, {{ill|Komparu|ja|金春流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komparu|1=Konparu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4525036}}, Kongō, and {{ill|Kita|ja|喜多流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita|1=Kita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11419173}}) that did not publish its own nō text collections. During the early Edo period, the Kita School separated from the Kongō School.
In the mid-Edo period, the {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|qid=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa Domain|1=Yonezawa Domain}} developed a strong connection with the Kongō School. The 8th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Shigetada|ja|上杉重定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uesugi Shigetada|1=Uesugi Shigesada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8514768}}, was a patron of the school. In {{nihongo|1778|安永7th year }}, the 9th daimyō, {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|qid=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}}, invited the then head, Kongō Saburō, to his domain. As a result, the Kongō School gained a following among the domain's samurai. To this day, the Yonezawa region remains the only area north of Tokyo with a significant Kongō School presence.
[[Kongō Yuiitsu]], active during the late Edo and early Meiji periods, is credited with introducing the thousand-threaded spider's web in the play ''{{ill|Tsuchigumo|en|Tsuchigumo|qid=Q2063089|lt=Tsuchigumo|1=Tsuchigumo}}''. In the late Edo period, the family resided in {{ill|Lawn|en|Lawn|qid=Q207766|lt=Shiba|1=Lawn}} {{ill|Iikura|ja|飯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iikura|1=iigura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11666115}}, and their stipend was 100 koku.
In 1936, with the death of [[Kongō Ukyō]], the Sakato-Kongō family line ended. In 1937, with the recommendation of the other four schools, [[Kongō Kinnosuke]], a master from the [[Nomura Kongō family]] in Kyoto, succeeded the Kongō family headship. His son, [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]], continued the tradition. Subsequent prominent figures include [[Toyoshima Yazaemon]] and [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]]. The current head, [[Kongō Hisanori]], assumed the position as the 26th head on September 18, 1998.
The Kongō School is the only shite-kata school based in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} and owns the '''Kongō Nōgakudō''': * Location: Karasuma-dōri Nakadachiuri, Kyoto * Seating capacity: 412 fixed seats, 80 auxiliary seats
== Lineage of Heads == * 14th [[Kongō Ryokushō]] * 15th [[Kongō Nagayori]] '''* Son of the 14th. * 17th [[Kongō Yaichihisanori]] * 18th [[Kongō Saburō Ujifuku]] '''* Eldest son of the 17th. * 19th [[Kongō Ujitada]] '''* Second son of the 17th; later adopted by the 18th. * 20th [[Kongō Saburō Ujie]] * 21st [[Kongō Yuiitsu]] '''* Second son of the 20th. * 22nd [[Kongō Ujiyoshi]] '''* Eldest son of the 21st. * 23rd [[Kongō Ukyō]] '''* Eldest son of the 22nd. * 24th [[Kongō Gō (1st)|1st Kongō Gō]] '''* Second son of [[Kongō Kinnosuke]]. * 25th [[Kongō Gō (2nd)|2nd Kongō Gō]] '''* Third son of the 24th. * 26th [[Kongō Hisanori]] '''* Eldest son of the 25th.
== Additional Notes == The Kongō School's Noh performances are said to have influenced {{ill|Kyomai|ja|京舞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyomai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上方舞|1=Kamigata-mai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11359080}}, particularly {{ill|Inoue School|ja|井上流|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue School|1=Inoue-ryū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11373112}}. Some Kongō performers have family ties to Inoue School dancers.<ref>''Nōgaku: Zeami, Kan'ami, Masterpieces. Komparu School "Takazago" Komparu Nobutaka / Kongō School "Kiyotsune" Hirota Heiichi,'' [[:en:NHK Enterprises|NHK Enterprises]].</ref>
